sentences.text: Your keys and the--
Yeah.
And [INAUDIBLE] does-- really can open lightly the door.
He should have been [INTERPOSING VOICES] knock.
Very lightly.
Quietly.
Harriet Solz, S-O-L-Z.
O-F-F-M-A-N.
By my Polish name, first name.
Henia.
H-E-N-I-A.
Now I'm 65.
Krakow, Poland.
K-R-A-K-O-W.
First of all, I was 10 years old when the war broke out to begin with.
And I had a younger sister, so-- and my parents.
We were four people in the house.
Hermina.
And I had a beautiful life as a child.
I really didn't know from nothing.
I went to dancing school, I took piano lessons, I went to the movies, went to the circus.
And, and that's-- when I was a, a real-- how shall I say?
I was a big baby--
[LAUGHS] --at that time because I was-- I came from a very strict home where the upbringing was very strict and sheltered life.
I wasn't allowed to go to a park by myself.
Even to school, the maid had to walk me to take me to school and pick me up from school, and so on.
I wasn't spoiled, I was sheltered I would say more.
Spoiled I wasn't because I had very strict upbringing.
The manners were very important in our house, table manners.
And-- only sheltered.
I wasn't-- I didn't have the freedom, which some children my age already had that freedom.
Alone to play in the streets, and so on, I wasn't allowed.
I was a baby.
[LAUGHS] A baby.
My father had a business, led a business.
And my mother never worked.
A store.
A store, With leather.
Oh, I used to come every day for five-- to get $0.05 for candies.
[LAUGHS] That's all.
Polish.
Now, when I went to school, which my career in school wasn't too long, I was learning Hebrew.
But otherwise, we spoke Polish a lot.
I think I was six years old, if I'm not mistake.
I know when they closed the schools for Jewish children, I started fourth grade, public school.
Oh, school.
What I remember-- first, I went to public school.
And then my parents took me out, and put me in a private school.
I don't know why they did it.
This I cannot tell you.
And I-- that private school was called Hebrew School, which I had difficulties with the Hebrew language, and I had to have a tutor to help me with it.
But nothing special, nothing really.
In the private school, no.
That was strictly Jewish school.
Yeah.
Yes.
I really don't recall it because I was never left alone.
Like I said, the maids used to take me to school and pick me up.
And then I had a next-door neighbor, which we played together.
And I don't recall any-- with non-Jewish children, for sure not because it was such a big anti-Semitism that we wouldn't mingle.
Either side wouldn't mingle.
And I-- I remember I went to the park skiing, so they broke my ski because I was Jewish.
This was before the war.
And I used to go with my father-- he used to take me ice skating-- to a private, Jewish ice skating rink.
And when the war broke out, they closed up the Jewish ice skating rink, so he took me to a mixed one, Gentiles and Jews.
So, me, I look always typical Jewish.
They almost broke my legs on the skating rink.
Here is a Jew skating, and this was my last experience of skating.
How it felt?
Terrible.
But we grew up in this atmosphere that the Jews are hated in Poland.
No.
I don't remember.
But we were mostly with Jewish people, Jewish relatives.
I hear-- I don't remember Krakow too well.
Like I said, I was too young, and I never went by myself, only with my parents for a walk.
And even when we went with my parents for a walk, me and my sister held hands together like that.
And we walk in front of the parents when we went to the parks, something like that.
But I hear had 80,000 Jews, Krakow, in a quarter million, I think, population.
But I'm not sure, don't quote me.
They were modern observant.
We observe all of the holidays, and-- but we had a kosher home.
But otherwise, we were very modern.
I went only on high holidays.
My father took me for the shofar.
But like this-- and there was reform temple, Which my mother sometimes used to take me for services.
But I never went.
You see, the Hebrew school, school was modern.
Wasn't religious, but was modern.
We had a kosher home, like I said.
I observe all the holidays.
And I actually was more religious than the whole household because my neighbor was religious, so I became religious on my own.
[LAUGHS].
That's when I got sick.
My father wanted to take a cab.
Not a cab.
We had the horses like on Fifth Avenue in Central Park.
So I said, I won't to ride on Saturday.
So he says, if you're sick, you're allowed.
I said no, and he had to carry me maybe for a mile or longer to take me home.
I was the one religious, really.
Do you have any memories of the Jewish holidays besides your--
Oh yeah.
The Passover, we always had the beautiful seder, which my-- my father was very traditional.
When came holidays, you would think he's very, very religious.
Everything was performed to a T.
But after the holidays, he went back to-- [LAUGHS] --to his ways.
So all of the holidays we celebrate and everything.
My earliest memory.
I really don't know what to say was my earliest memory.
Oh yeah, oh yeah.
Happy family.
Like I said, sheltered child, going to dressmakers to have dresses made up.
And going to family's for holidays, for dinners.
And playing with dolls.
I had a Shirley Temple doll already then, which was a big thing in Poland.
So I really had a good life, going to-- for summer vacation.
We used to go away, every summer away.
[SIGHS] We went, the name [NON-ENGLISH] that was a resort, [BACKGROUND NOISE] and we went a few times there Then we went to [NON-ENGLISH] another resort.
And as a matter of fact, before the war broke out, I was never happy about going away from Krakow.
For me was-- I never liked to leave Krakow.
As a matter of fact, I supposed to go to a camp overnight. 1939, supposed to go to camp, in the place, [NON-ENGLISH], something like-- I don't remember the name.
But there were talks of the war, so my parents were afraid to send me.
But I don't think I would have enjoyed it.
I'm not the camping type [LAUGHS].
I was 10 years old.
I'll tell you.
I am ashamed to admit, but I'll tell you the honest truth.
You know, a child mind works differently than grownups.
And I have a granddaughter who is 10 years old, so I always watch her and compare.
When I heard the war broke out, I was happy.
It's a shame to admit because I used to study geography, and Poland was in other wars.
So I thought I'll sit by the window, and I'll watch them fighting.
So I remember when the war broke out.
Later on, the soldiers would park in the park when they came in to Krakow.
I ran to the park to look at them.
And to me-- to me, this was-- I said, they're not so bad, what people were talking.
But what did I know about?
What did you hear and see that day?
Do you remember?
Nothing special, nothing special.
But later on, it started.
They started regular procedure with, with cutting down places where Jews can go-- can go.
Not every place we were allowed to walk.
And we had to wear armbands, Jewish armbands.
But I didn't have to because I was too young.
It started at age 13.
But I was jealous because everybody wears a Jewish armband.
See how a child mind [BACKGROUND NOISE] works?
So I used to take from home the armband, and put-- once I left the house, I put it on my arm to walk around with it.
What color was the armbands that you had?
It was-- it was white.
White with a blue Jewish star.
No, my father was in the army when the war broke out.
And he-- but he came when the war finished.
He was taken to prison, and then they released him as a prisoner, war prisoner.
And they released him.
He came to Krakow.
Some once my father came, of course, the situation at home improved.
I don't remember.
It took few weeks because when the war started, we stood in line for bread and everything.
But my father came, and he came home with two big breads, which was a very big relief.
And he was with us all the time.
Then I would say the war broke out in September.
October, November they closed schools for Jewish children.
Jewish children weren't allowed to go to school.
Me again, I was happy.
I won't have to go to school.
I'm ashamed to admit, but that's the way I felt.
But my-- to my father, education was the most important thing.
And he organized with few other parents who have children the same age when we went to school.
And we were studying in hiding.
If they would have caught us, they would have killed us.
And we were maybe five or six girls, which were studying.
And we had three professors on different subjects.
And I, I completed public school because, like I would say, within two, three months, the course completed in a way.
So I completed public school.
And this went through to ghetto and the first year of gymnasium.
And I remember my father went to see the teacher, how I am doing.
So he says I don't know if I'll promote your daughter to second year gymnasium because she's very bad in Latin.
She's falling.
[LAUGHS] Failing.
In those times, in such a-- [LAUGHS] But the education was taken serious.
So I was-- I know this.
So I have to study, other children don't have to.
But in the long run, he did the right thing.
I, I never realized what this means.
Oh yeah.
They were scared about us, because me and my sister, we were very young.
And children were taken away.
Children were killed.
And they always shivered.
What's going to happen to us?
German soldier.
That-- first of all, whenever they've had their chance, they try to kill somebody.
And especially religious Jews, they used to pull out the locks, the side locks with the skin.
And we used to have to clean the streets, snow off the streets.
And in Poland, we have lot of snow.
And, and the struggle what was going on.
They had-- they had black, which was SS.
They had yellow, which was SA called.
They were the murders.
And then the army uniform, green.
Green color.
Action started?
I recall it first in ghetto, really.
Their actions, the real actions of-- they-- and we knew about that, as they come and people were on the list.
At night, they picked them up.
They took them to Auschwitz.
But we were still in our houses in Krakow at that time.
And then they started.
We have to bring our furs.
Whoever had the fur, had to bring to the main market to, to give up the furs, and so on.
But their actions, as I recall it, started in ghetto, what I remember.
And that time, we went to ghetto.
[SIGHS] Like Spielberg made a fantastic scene showing people leaving Krakow.
We dragged our belongings with us.
We thought, God knows what we going to-- and we'll be able to keep whatever you were able to pack up and push on the-- on the carts.
You pushed the belongings with you, and you took it to ghetto.
But in the meanwhile, we came to ghetto, the facilities weren't what were-- what you were used to.
The apartments.
At the beginning, we got one room with a kitchen for four people, which was a luxury.
So you had no room.
My mother, I remember, took her paintings with her to hang up, but there was no room where to do it.
In ghetto, really, start all the actions.
And constantly, they, they came to the houses, check.
And luckily, I was tall for my age, so I got away.
Which I-- we had false papers, me and my sister made, as we are much older than we were.
So when the-- at the beginning, they used to look at your papers, the IDs.
So we got away.
Sometimes we were hidden under the beds because depends on what kind of action was going on.
How frequent were these?
They were quite often, really quite often.
Big actions.
And most of the people went.
They took them to [NON-ENGLISH] And from there, they would send them to Belzec, to [INAUDIBLE] Belzec.
They-- my parents got for me.
I wasn't-- I couldn't get it for my own.
I wouldn't know even what to do.
Oh yeah.
This, everything I remembered.
Yeah.
Oh, the same names.
Polish name, what was on my birth certificate, which was Henrika.
And that's the name.
Only the dates are changed.
To-- that's the best-- I saw the I didn't remember that, and I never knew.
My sister was born in 1931, and I was born in 1929.
So we are both marked born 1927, within two weeks [LAUGHS].
I was born May 1, she was born May 15.
I have the list.
But-- and you live constantly in the fear.
You didn't know what next day going to bring.
You didn't think that you're going to live next day.
Oh yeah.
And I tell you, I am left still today with so much fear in my body that you can't imagine.
I was always very scared, very unsure of myself.
And I thought, maybe if I would be more mature, I would feel differently.
But very scared.
And to today's day, when I see policeman, I'm scared.
I can't walk in, like, to a bank.
My husband has to go with me.
Of any official offices, I can't walk.
I get anxiety at this.
I can't walk in, I'm frightened.
And I am frightened to sign any legal papers, to put my name to it.
My father-- for awhile, it was-- since we had the leather business, they combined all the leather stores, and they made one big store.
So my father was managing-like, and he was going out of the ghetto to work.
Since he was going out of the ghetto, even though always something was able to smuggle in to the-- to the house.
So he was working for awhile in-- outside the ghetto in Krakow.
But later on, in Krakow-- I mean, in the ghetto.
In ghetto?
Yeah.
I still was getting till the when they had the less action, which was brutal.
Lot of people were killed and, and sent out.
So I was for awhile in the ghetto.
We were from 1941 through '43 in ghetto.
So I would say for a year, maybe, was receiving schooling.
Were you were about to celebrate Jewish holidays at all in the ghetto?
I don't recall it.
Oh, there were barbed wires, no question about it.
And the ghetto was getting, every day, smaller and smaller.
We lived with my aunt, and uncle and their children.
We're, maybe, 10 people in one room at the end.
And everybody had a nail hanging over their bed to know-- to have room where to put their clothes because in the morning, they shouldn't look for it.
The conditions were terrible.
And the toilets were outside.
I don't know how we managed that.
[LAUGHS].
[BACKGROUND NOISE]
Food-- I think they gave us rations.
Oh yeah, oh yeah.
Sure, I remember being hungry and everything.
But still again, I was stupid child things that's going to--
So we were in ghetto, ghetto B, actually.
And ghetto A was for people who were able to work.
Ghetto B was for families with children.
Which later on, this was designed to go to Auschwitz to the ovens.
But when they were liquidating ghetto, my father was already in Plaszow.
How, what?
Don't ask me, I don't know.
But he was in Plaszow.
And I remember my mother was packing again those big--
[LAUGHS] --big straw baskets to take to-- that's because they said we're going to take you to a nice place.
They didn't say they going to take us to the ovens.
And we were in ghetto B where people didn't work there.
There were old people and children.
So my father came, smuggle himself into ghetto.
He came running, and he pulled us out from ghetto B in the last minute.
He was in Plaszow?
He was already in Plaszow.
Smuggled.
People smuggled ways.
What?
Yeah.
They-- you know, people tried to smuggle through, finagle.
You'd be surprised how people used to.
They smuggle in children in suitcases going to, to Plaszow.
Because children weren't allowed to go, so they put them in knapsacks and suitcases.
You became shrewd.
You had to fight for your life.
So my father smuggled himself through.
Of course, he risked his life.
Everything, whatever you did, you risked your life.
If it went through, fine, You were OK, you were a hero.
So he pulls us from ghetto B in the last minute.
And he dressed us up in my mother coats, long coats with high heels, that we should look older, they shouldn't stop us at the gate going to Plaszow because Goth was watching every face.
Goth was the biggest murderer in Plaszow.
So me, such a spoiled child, I went to take-- you wouldn't believe it worked-- a strainer.
What is a strainer?
In Poland, you boiled milk, so there was always a skin on top of the milk a skin foam from boiling.
And I wouldn't drink milk if the skin was floating.
So I had my personal strainer over a glass, which you put it, and you poured the milk through so whole skin was left on the strainer.
I took with me to Plaszow in case I get milk.
How will I be able to drink it?
[LAUGHS]
I think couple miles.
I don't remember.
It was a long walk, but how long, far, I really don't know.
I don't know.
Maybe 20,000 or more.
I don't know.
March 13, yeah.
They liquidated Krakow.
So Saturday, we went.
They took us-- we managed to get to Plaszow.
